product.name: Capdrop
product.tagline: Drink water. Earn caps. Keep the streak alive.

hero.headline: A water tracker built for one-tap follow-through.
hero.subhead: Capdrop turns each drink into visible bottle progress, bottle-cap rewards, widgets, reminders, and a streak map that makes hydration hard to ignore.

summary: Capdrop is a visual iOS water tracker with quick logging, bottle-cap rewards, widgets, reminders, optional HealthKit writes, and exportable history.
audience: People who want a very low-friction water habit
Visual-first users who need progress to feel obvious
Users who prefer local data instead of accounts or cloud dashboards
coreProblems: Water tracking apps often ask for too much attention
Progress can feel abstract when it is only a number
Reminders are easy to dismiss unless logging is immediate

features.title: One-tap logging
features.summary: Log common amounts from the home screen, widgets, or notification actions.

features.title: Bottle-first progress
features.summary: A large animated bottle shows the current fill and completed bottles without making you read a dashboard.

features.title: Bottle-cap rewards
features.summary: Each 500 mL earns a cap, giving the habit a small visual reward loop.

features.title: Widgets
features.summary: Small, medium, large, and Lock Screen widgets show progress and support quick logging.

features.title: Local history
features.summary: Daily totals, weekly bars, streaks, and a contribution-style hydration map stay on device.

features.title: Optional HealthKit
features.summary: Capdrop can write water entries to Apple Health when the user enables access.

workflows.title: Drink, tap, move on
workflows.summary: The home screen keeps the bottle, amount, caps, and quick buttons in one reach-friendly view.

workflows.title: Use widgets as shortcuts
workflows.summary: Log from the home screen widget when opening an app would create friction.

workflows.title: Review the week visually
workflows.summary: Weekly bars and the dot map make missed days and strong days obvious.

proofPoints.label: Storage
proofPoints.note: No account or backend is required.

proofPoints.label: Widgets
proofPoints.note: Small, medium, large, and Lock Screen families.

proofPoints.label: Privacy
proofPoints.note: No ads, analytics, or third-party SDKs in the native app.

faq.question: Does Capdrop need an account?
faq.answer: No. The app is designed to work locally on the device.

faq.question: Does Capdrop send hydration data to a server?
faq.answer: No backend is used by the native app. Optional exports are created only when the user chooses to share a local JSON file.

faq.question: Does it integrate with Apple Health?
faq.answer: Yes, optional HealthKit write access is available for water entries when the user grants permission.

faq.question: Are widgets animated?
faq.answer: Widgets use lightweight snapshots to respect iOS battery and WidgetKit limits. The live filling animation is inside the app.

screenshots.caption: Home: bottle progress, caps, and one-tap logging.

screenshots.alt: Capdrop stats screen with weekly bars and hydration constellation
screenshots.caption: Stats: weekly progress, streak, and yearly dot map.

screenshots.alt: Capdrop nudges screen with reminder modes
screenshots.caption: Nudges: optional reminder modes and notification actions.

screenshots.alt: Capdrop Studio screen with visual features and bottle moods
screenshots.caption: Studio: themes, widgets, and visual motivation tools.

screenshots.alt: Capdrop settings screen with app icon picker
screenshots.caption: Settings: goal, units, and selectable app icons.

catalog.title: Capdrop
catalog.blurb: A visual iOS water tracker with bottle progress, cap rewards, widgets, reminders, and streak maps.

repoArchetypeReasoning: The repo contains an iOS SwiftUI app under ios/App with WidgetKit, AppIntents, notifications, HealthKit, local storage, and app icon assets.
The older Vite/React PWA remains in the repo, but current launch work is centered on the native iOS target.
assumptions: App Store URL is not final yet, so launch CTA points to the public repository until an App Store link exists.
Support uses the repository issues page unless a dedicated support email is added later.
missingButUsefulAssets: Production App Store screenshots after device QA
